museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Ultima III appears, the Acorn Electron launches, the Famicom settles into its first weeks, and Britain's home-computer culture keeps turning bedrooms into small arcades.
museum.curatorNote: August balances verified hardware and RPG anchors with UK seasonal context. Some Spectrum titles are included as 1983 context where exact month is not secure.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: August makes the home computer feel like an institution.

items.title: Ultima III: Exodus is released
items.body: Origin's RPG broadened party-based computer role-playing and became a major influence on later Japanese and western RPG design.

items.title: Acorn launches the Electron
items.body: The Electron brought Acorn's educational reputation to a cheaper home market, though production shortages would complicate its first Christmas.

items.title: Famicom's early life begins unevenly
items.body: Nintendo's new machine was out in Japan, but early hardware issues would later force attention and repair work.
items.sourceNote: Famicom histories describe early hardware problems and 1983 recall/repair context.

items.title: The UK micro market crowds further
items.body: Spectrum, BBC, C64, Dragon, VIC-20 and now Electron gave families too many plausible computers and no simple answer.

items.title: Computer RPGs feel bigger than arcade translations
items.body: Ultima III showed a different path from the arcade: maps, parties, persistence and a whole imagined world.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight August shelf objects and nearby releases.

items.title: Ultima III: Exodus
items.body: A party-based RPG milestone with a world broad enough to invite note-taking and obsession.

items.title: Acorn Electron
items.body: A lower-cost Acorn machine aimed at UK homes and schools-adjacent buyers.

items.title: M.U.L.E.
items.body: EA's economic multiplayer design continued proving computer games could be social and cerebral.

items.title: Archon
items.body: A board game that turned into an action game whenever pieces met.

items.title: Manic Miner
items.body: Now part of the active UK conversation: difficult, colourful and instantly recognisable.

items.title: Jetpac
items.body: Still fresh enough to define Ultimate as a name worth following.

items.title: Dragon's Lair
items.body: The cabinet's novelty kept drawing attention even where the play model divided opinion.

items.title: Famicom launch games
items.body: Donkey Kong, Donkey Kong Jr. and Popeye formed the machine's first public software identity.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: August is full of keyboards and early console signals.

items.title: Acorn Electron
items.body: A cheaper Acorn machine promising BBC-like respectability at home, with games as part of the unspoken appeal.

items.title: Apple II
items.body: Ultima III's original home gave RPG players disk drives, maps and long-form play.

items.title: Famicom
items.body: The new Japanese console was still early, exciting and technically not fully settled.

items.title: ZX Spectrum
items.body: By August, the Spectrum felt like Britain's fast-moving games machine.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: August covers could sell both seriousness and play.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: Computer worlds beyond the arcade
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for August 1983.
items.curatorNote: Ultima III belonged to a longer, slower kind of play than most cabinet coverage.
items.body: Ultima III belonged to a longer, slower kind of play than most cabinet coverage.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Electron, Spectrum and the crowded UK shelf
items.curatorNote: A new Acorn machine was hardware news and games news at once.
items.body: A new Acorn machine was hardware news and games news at once.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: Which home computer belongs in the house?
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for August 1983.
items.curatorNote: The UK buying decision was no longer simple, and the magazines became guides to competing futures.
items.body: The UK buying decision was no longer simple, and the magazines became guides to competing futures.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: Spectrum games gather identity
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for August 1983.
items.curatorNote: The Spectrum had moved from cheap curiosity to a platform with recognisable hits.
items.body: The Spectrum had moved from cheap curiosity to a platform with recognisable hits.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: August's network involved maps, notes and schoolyard tapes.

items.title: RPG knowledge was handwritten
items.body: Ultima III encouraged notebooks, maps and shared discoveries.

items.title: New hardware arrived as questions
items.body: The Electron was something to compare, not simply buy.

items.title: Early console news lagged across borders
items.body: The Famicom mattered hugely, but not yet in everyday UK play.

items.title: Cassette swapping shaped memory
items.body: Games travelled with handwriting, photocopied instructions and trust.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: August felt like choosing a machine meant choosing a future.

items.title: The RPG felt vast
items.body: Ultima III made home play feel bigger than a score chase.

items.title: The Electron promised respectability
items.body: It had the Acorn name, the school aura and the games potential.

items.title: The Spectrum felt impatient
items.body: New software seemed to arrive faster than anyone could master it.

items.title: Japan's console story was beginning elsewhere
items.body: The Famicom was important, but still remote for most UK readers.
